content:
# Genesis Chapter 17 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 9v–10r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_017_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_017_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0016

---

## The Covenant Cut into the Body

**1** When Abram was ninety-nine years old, the LORD appeared to him and said: "I am El Shaddai — God Almighty. Walk before me, and be whole-hearted. **2** I will make my covenant between me and you, and I will multiply you greatly."

**3** Abram fell on his face, and God spoke to him:

**4** "As for me — here is my covenant with you: you will become the father of a multitude of nations. **5** Your name will no longer be Abram. Your name will be Abraham, for I have made you the father of a multitude of nations. **6** I will make you extremely fruitful. I will make nations come from you, and kings will descend from you. **7** I will establish my covenant between me and you, and between me and your descendants after you, throughout their generations — an everlasting covenant: to be God to you and to your descendants after you. **8** And I will give to you — and to your descendants after you — the land where you are now a foreigner, all the land of Canaan, as an everlasting possession. And I will be their God."

**9** Then God said to Abraham, "As for you — you shall keep my covenant, you and your descendants after you throughout their generations. **10** This is my covenant which you shall keep, between me and you and your descendants after you: every male among you shall be circumcised. **11** You shall circumcise the flesh of your foreskin, and it will be the sign of the covenant between me and you. **12** Throughout your generations, every male among you shall be circumcised when he is eight days old — every male born in your household and every male bought with your money from any foreigner who is not your offspring. **13** Both the one born in your household and the one bought with your money must be circumcised. My covenant will be in your flesh as an everlasting covenant. **14** Any uncircumcised male — one who has not had the flesh of his foreskin circumcised — that person shall be cut off from his people. He has broken my covenant."

**15** Then God said to Abraham, "As for Sarai your wife — you shall no longer call her Sarai. Her name will be Sarah. **16** I will bless her, and I will also give you a son by her. I will bless her, and she will become mother to nations. Kings of peoples will come from her."

**17** Abraham fell on his face and laughed. He said to himself, *"Can a child really be born to a man who is a hundred years old? And can Sarah — at ninety years old — really give birth?"*

**18** Then Abraham said to God, "If only Ishmael might live before you!"

**19** But God said, "No — your wife Sarah will bear you a son, and you shall call his name Isaac. I will establish my covenant with him as an everlasting covenant for his descendants after him. **20** And as for Ishmael — I have heard you. I will bless him and make him fruitful and multiply him greatly. He will father twelve princes, and I will make him into a great nation. **21** But my covenant I will establish with Isaac, whom Sarah will bear to you at this appointed time next year." **22** When he had finished speaking with him, God went up from Abraham.

**23** Abraham took his son Ishmael, and every male born in his household and every male bought with his money — every male in Abraham's house — and that very same day he circumcised the flesh of their foreskins, exactly as God had told him. **24** Abraham was ninety-nine years old when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin. **25** And his son Ishmael was thirteen years old when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin. **26** That very same day Abraham was circumcised, and so was his son Ishmael. **27** And all the men of his household — those born in the house and those bought with money from foreigners — were circumcised with him.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Genesis 17 — the covenant that breaks skin

Thirteen years of silence.

That is the gap the text creates between the end of Genesis 16 and the beginning of Genesis 17. Abram was eighty-six when Ishmael was born. He is ninety-nine when God appears in this chapter. Thirteen years without a recorded word. No theophany. No covenant ceremony. No vision in the night. Just Abram, growing older, with his son — the son the slave-woman bore him — and the promise that has not moved.

The silence is not accidental. The narrative is doing something with time. When God reappears, everything changes: a new divine name, a new covenant sign, two new human names, and a prophecy that, after all this waiting, leaves Abraham on the ground laughing.

---

### Verse 1 — El Shaddai: the name that comes with command

**וַיֵּרָא יְהוָה אֶל-אַבְרָם** — "The LORD appeared to Abram." After thirteen years. The first word of the encounter is not a word — it is an appearance. God shows up. The Hebrew verb **רָאָה** in the Niphal — *to be seen, to appear* — describes a theophany, a moment when the divine becomes visible or perceptible. It is the same verb used in Genesis 12:7 and 26:2. God is not merely heard; God is seen.

The first thing God says is a name: **אֲנִי אֵל שַׁדַּי** — "I am El Shaddai."

This is the introduction of a new divine name, and it has generated centuries of discussion about what it means. The traditional rendering is "God Almighty" — used consistently in the Septuagint (which translates it **θεὸς σαδδαι** or **παντοκράτωρ**, "the all-powerful") and in Jerome's Vulgate (*omnipotens*). But the etymology of **שַׁדַּי** (*Shaddai*) is genuinely uncertain.

Several proposals are active in the scholarly tradition. The most commonly cited is from the Akkadian *shadu* — "mountain" — suggesting that El Shaddai may originally have meant "God of the mountain," connoting the mountain deity of the Canaanite highlands. A second proposal, developed in modern scholarship, derives it from the Hebrew **שָׁד** (*shad*) — "breast" — making El Shaddai "God of breasts" or "God who nourishes," a name emphasizing divine fertility and sustaining power. This reading fits the context strikingly: the name is introduced precisely in the chapter that promises Abraham fatherhood of nations, and it returns in the patriarchal narratives whenever blessing and fertility are at issue (Genesis 28:3; 35:11; 43:14). A third proposal derives it from a root meaning "destroyer" or "the one who overwhelms." The LXX's **παντοκράτωρ** — the one who holds all power — synthesizes several of these senses.

What is clear from context is that the name carries weight of power and abundance. Whatever its precise etymology, El Shaddai is the name through which Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob encounter divine blessing and multiplication. It is the name of the God who is able to make the barren fruitful, the aged new, the impossible actual.

The command follows immediately: **הִתְהַלֵּךְ לְפָנַי וֶהְיֵה תָמִים** — "Walk before me and be whole-hearted."

**הִתְהַלֵּךְ** — *hithallekh* — the Hithpael of *halakh*, "to walk": it is continuous, reflexive motion. Not "walk" once, but walk in an ongoing manner, in a certain orientation, before a certain presence. The same construction was used of Enoch (Genesis 5:22, 24) and of Noah (Genesis 6:9). To "walk before God" is to conduct the whole of one's life in awareness of divine presence — not the performance of occasional religious observance but an entire orientation of existence.

**תָמִים** — *tamim* — is variously translated "perfect," "blameless," "complete," "whole-hearted," "upright." It is the word used of an unblemished sacrificial animal. Applied to a person, it does not mean sinless in the modern perfectionist sense — it means integrated, undivided, without split loyalty. The same word will be used to describe Noah (Genesis 6:9). It is the quality of a life that does not try to serve two masters, does not keep one room locked from God while opening the others. It is the opposite of moral fragmentation.

The call is both simple and total: *walk continuously before me, and be whole*.

---

### Verses 2–8 — the covenant: God's self-binding

**וְנָתְנָה בְרִיתִי בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ** — "And I will give my covenant between me and you."

The language of covenant (**בְּרִית**, *berit*) runs through this entire chapter like a spine: it appears thirteen times in twenty-seven verses. Every structural movement of the chapter is marked by it. Covenant is the form of this encounter.

But notice the syntax. God does not say "we will make a covenant." He says "I will give (*natan*) my covenant." The covenant is God's to give. It is not a negotiated treaty between equals; it is a unilateral commitment from the sovereign to the dependent, from the Eternal to the mortal. Abraham's part will come — but it comes in response to what God initiates. The direction of grace runs from God toward the human, not the other way.

Verses 4 through 8 are God's side of the covenant, presented in a cascade of first-person divine promises — "I will make," "I will establish," "I will give," "I will be." Counted carefully, there are seven first-person divine commitments in these verses. Seven is the number of completeness and oath in the Semitic world. God binds himself, seven-fold, to Abraham's future.

The content of the promises: fruitfulness, nations, kings, land, and — most foundationally — relationship. **וְהָיִיתִי לָהֶם לֵאלֹהִים** — "I will be God to them." This is the covenant formula that will echo through the entire biblical tradition, from Moses to the prophets to the book of Revelation: "I will be their God and they shall be my people." Its origin is here, spoken to Abraham in Canaan. All the land and nations and kings matter, but underneath them is this: the God who made everything intends to be in personal relationship with this family, and through this family with the world.

---

### Verses 5 and 15 — name changes: identity transformation

**וְלֹא-יִקָּרֵא עוֹד אֶת-שִׁמְךָ אַבְרָם וְהָיָה שִׁמְךָ אַבְרָהָם** — "Your name shall no longer be called Abram; your name shall be Abraham."

The name change is the covenant's seal. In the ancient world, a name was not a label — it was an identity. To rename someone was to reconstitute who they were, to speak their new reality into existence. God names the man who will become the father of nations, and the name itself carries the claim.

**אַבְרָם** (*Abram*) most likely means "exalted father" — *ab* (father) + *ram* (high, exalted). **אַבְרָהָם** (*Abraham*) is explained by the text itself as meaning "father of a multitude" — **כִּי אַב-הֲמוֹן גּוֹיִם נְתַתִּיךָ** — "for I have made you the father of a multitude (*hamon*) of nations." The problem is that this is not a true etymology. *Abraham* and *hamon* share a loose phonetic resemblance but are not related by standard derivation. The text is doing something other than lexicography. It is doing theology through sound — reaching for the word that rhymes with "father of multitudes" in order to declare what this man now is. The name is not explained; it is proclaimed. God speaks the future into Abraham's name and tells him to wear it.

The same logic governs Sarah's renaming. **שָׂרַי** (*Sarai*) and **שָׂרָה** (*Sarah*) are almost certainly variant forms of the same word — both mean "princess" or "noblewoman." The change is a single letter, a shift in vowel pointing, but the meaning is reoriented from particular to universal. Sarai — *my princess*, a name with a possessive flavor — becomes Sarah — *princess*, *noblewoman*, a title without a limiting suffix. She is no longer defined by her relationship to one man's household. She is the mother of nations, and her name belongs to a wider inheritance.

In both cases the name change is the visible, speakable form of what God is doing. Every time someone calls these names from this day forward, they are participating in the covenant announcement. The community around Abraham and Sarah will speak the promise into existence every time they open their mouths.

---

### Verses 9–14 — circumcision: the sign cut into flesh

**וְאַתָּה אֶת-בְּרִיתִי תִשְׁמֹר** — "And as for you — you shall keep my covenant."

After seven divine commitments, Abraham is given one human obligation: circumcision. The asymmetry is worth sitting with. God binds himself with fruitfulness, nations, kings, land, and eternal relationship. Abraham is asked to cut the flesh of every male in his household.

**הִמּוֹל לָכֶם כָּל-זָכָר** — "every male among you shall be circumcised." The word **מוּל** (*mul*), "to circumcise," or more literally "to cut," appears again and again in these verses. The act is physical, permanent, irreversible. The sign of the covenant is not worn on the body — it is in the body. It is not like a ring that can be removed or a tattoo that could theoretically be covered. It is written into anatomy itself.

Why circumcision? The text does not explain. It simply commands. But the choice is theologically rich in ways the narrative rewards:

First, the sign involves the organ of generation. The covenant promise is about offspring — nations, kings, descendants without number. The sign of that promise is placed precisely at the site of its future fulfillment. The body part through which the covenant will be continued is the body part marked by the covenant. There is a kind of seriousness in this that formal symbols — banners, altars, declarations — cannot match. This is not symbolic; this is anatomical.

Second, the sign requires pain and vulnerability. Covenant in the ancient Near East was sealed by cutting — animals cut in half in Genesis 15, the covenanting parties passing between the pieces. The word **בְּרִית** (*berit*, covenant) may itself derive from the verb "to cut." Circumcision is the cutting of the covenant seal into the body of every covenant member. It is not a comfortable rite. It costs something. And it marks the cost permanently.

Third — and the text makes this explicit — the sign is communal and inclusive. Not only Abraham. Not only his biological descendants. Every male in his household, including slaves purchased from foreigners, must bear the sign. The covenant community is defined not by bloodline alone but by the mark in the body. This is a radical social claim: the covenant is open beyond the biological family.

The threat of verse 14 is stark and, as the manifest notes, linguistically ironic: **וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא מֵעַמֶּיהָ** — "that person shall be *cut off* (*karet*) from his people." The verb used for the penalty of covenant-breaking — *karet*, to be cut off — is the same root as **כָּרַת**, to cut a covenant. The uncircumcised man who has not been cut with the covenant-cut will be cut from the community. The punishment mirrors the missed ritual. To refuse the cutting is to receive a different and more terrible cutting.

---

### Verse 17 — Abraham's laughter: two things at once

**וַיִּפֹּל אַבְרָהָם עַל-פָּנָיו וַיִּצְחָק** — "Abraham fell on his face and laughed."

The syntax is breathtaking. Abraham falls — an act of prostration, of reverence, of overwhelmed response to the divine. And in the same breath, the same motion: he laughs.

The verb **צָחַק** (*tsachaq*) is the laugh-root — the same root that will become the name Isaac (*Yitschaq* — "he laughs"). This is the first of two laughs in the patriarchal narrative: Abraham's here, and Sarah's in Genesis 18:12. Both laugh at the same impossibility. Both laugh at their own bodies, at what time has done to them, at the audacity of the promise in the face of biology.

**בְּלִבּוֹ** — "in his heart," "to himself." Abraham does not laugh in God's face. He falls on his face and, in the privacy of his own interior, laughs. He thinks: *"Can a child really be born to a man who is a hundred years old? Can Sarah give birth at ninety?"* The question is not defiant. It is overwhelmed. It is the laughter of a man standing at the edge of the impossible, unable to keep a straight face.

The tradition has sometimes read Abraham's laugh as doubt and Sarah's (in chapter 18) as the faithless version — she laughs and is challenged about it, while Abraham is not challenged. But this reading oversimplifies both. Abraham's laughter does not prevent his obedience; he will carry out the covenant sign on that very same day. Sarah's laughter, in chapter 18, is also an understandable human response to the same impossibility. Both laugh. Both are right to laugh. And both are wrong about the limits of what God can do.

The laugh is not condemned. It is preserved. And it is honored with a name: the child born of this impossible promise will be named for the laughter it provoked. Every time someone says *Isaac*, they are saying *he laughed*. The doubt and the joy and the astonishment and the impossibility are all sealed into a name, and the name is kept forever.

---

### Verse 18 — Abraham's love for Ishmael

**וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם אֶל-הָאֱלֹהִים לוּ יִשְׁמָעֵאל יִחְיֶה לְפָנֶיךָ** — "And Abraham said to God: 'If only Ishmael might live before you!'"

This is one of the most tender verses in the entire book of Genesis.

God has just announced that Sarah will bear a son. Abraham, still on his face — having just laughed at the impossibility of this — turns to the child he already has and pleads for him. He does not argue with the announcement of Isaac. He does not protest the preference for the future child over the present one. He simply says: *lù* — "if only." The particle **לוּ** is the particle of longing, of wishing, of prayer hovering at the edge of what can be asked. It is the vocabulary of a father who loves the son he has.

Abraham has raised Ishmael for thirteen years. He is not a distant patriarch regarding the slave-woman's child with detachment. He is a father. And when God announces a new covenant son, Abraham's instinct is not to ask about his own future or his status or what this means for his household. His instinct is: *what about my boy?*

**יִחְיֶה לְפָנֶיךָ** — "might live before you." The phrase **לִפְנֵי** (*lifney*, before the face of) is the language of presence, of access, of relationship. Abraham is not merely asking that Ishmael survive. He is asking that Ishmael have a place in God's presence — that his son be seen, known, kept. He wants for Ishmael what every parent wants for their child: that the child would be held in something larger than himself, known by someone who will not forget him.

God hears this. Verse 20 makes it explicit: **וְלִישְׁמָעֵאל שְׁמַעְתִּיךָ** — "And as for Ishmael — I have heard you." God acknowledges Abraham's plea directly, by the father's own words. The name *Ishmael* means "God hears" — and in this moment the meaning of the name is performed in the narrative: Abraham asks for his son Ishmael, and God says "I have heard you." The name and the act converge. Ishmael will be blessed, multiplied, and made a great nation. He will not be left outside.

But — **וְאֶת-בְּרִיתִי אָקִים אֶת-יִצְחָק** — "But my covenant I will establish with Isaac." The tenderness of God's response to Abraham's love for Ishmael does not change the direction of the covenant. God honors the father's heart and holds the covenant's course at once. Both things are true and neither cancels the other.

---

### Verses 19–21 — Isaac named before birth

**וְקָרָאתָ אֶת-שְׁמוֹ יִצְחָק** — "And you shall call his name Isaac."

As with Ishmael in chapter 16, the child is named before birth. The name — *Yitschaq*, "he laughs" — is chosen by God and commanded to Abraham. The laugh that Abraham laughed in verse 17 becomes, by divine decree, the name of the child. This is one of the more remarkable acts in the entire chapter: God takes the spontaneous human response to an impossible promise — laughter — and folds it into the name of the promise's fulfillment. The laughter is not rebuked. It is enshrined.

Every subsequent generation who calls this child's name will be saying, without perhaps knowing it: *the one who was laughed at, the one over whom a father laughed in disbelief, the one whose very existence seemed like a joke.* The name carries the history of the promise's reception: not faith triumphant but faith struggling with impossibility, and God naming the struggle good.

**בַּמּוֹעֵד הַזֶּה בַּשָּׁנָה הָאַחֶרֶת** — "at this appointed time next year." The word **מוֹעֵד** (*moed*) is significant. It is not merely "time" in the sense of chronological moment. It is appointed time — set time, fixed time, time that God has designated. The same word is used for the sacred appointed times of the festival calendar, for the seasons of divine meeting. God is saying: there is an appointment already on the calendar of eternity. Sarah will be at that appointment. The child will be born on divine schedule, not human biology's schedule.

---

### Verses 23–27 — immediate obedience: that very same day

**וַיִּקַּח אַבְרָהָם אֶת-יִשְׁמָעֵאל בְּנוֹ ... וַיָּמָל אֶת-בְּשַׂר עָרְלָתָם בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה** — "Abraham took Ishmael his son... and he circumcised the flesh of their foreskins on that very same day."

**בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה** — "on that very same day." The phrase appears three times in the closing verses of the chapter (vv. 23, 26, 27 — and it is the phrase used for the Exodus itself in Exodus 12:17, 41, 51). Abraham does not wait. He does not delay, deliberate, or defer. God speaks and Abraham acts — and he acts immediately, on the day he receives the command.

The arithmetic of the moment deserves attention. Abraham is ninety-nine. He circumcises himself. He also circumcises Ishmael, who is thirteen. And he circumcises every male in his household — the sons born in his house and the men purchased with his money. This is a large company of people. And Abraham does it all on the same day God speaks to him.

The chapter that began in encounter and covenant ends in action and pain. The intimacy of bodily covenant, noted in the task brief, is at its most literal here: the sign of God's promise is cut into the most private and vulnerable part of every male in Abraham's household. There is nothing abstract about this. No ceremony at a safe remove, no symbolic gesture, no representative act. The covenant comes home to every body.

Ishmael's circumcision is noted specifically: he is thirteen years old. This detail is preserved in the Islamic tradition as the basis for performing circumcision at puberty rather than infancy. The text itself gives no preference — both infancy (Genesis 17:12 specifies eight days for future generations) and Ishmael's thirteen years are recorded without hierarchy. But the specificity of Ishmael's age in the narrative is the text's way of insisting that Ishmael, too, bears the covenant mark. He is not merely Abraham's son who was displaced by Isaac. He is a circumcised son of Abraham, marked with the sign of the covenant, included in the community that the sign defines.

---

### The chapter's three movements

Genesis 17 has a structural clarity unusual even for a chapter as architecturally deliberate as Genesis:

**Movement One (vv. 1–8): God's covenant with Abraham.** God speaks. El Shaddai is introduced. Abraham is renamed. Seven divine commitments are made. The direction of grace runs entirely from God to Abraham.

**Movement Two (vv. 9–14): Circumcision as the covenant sign.** The human response is defined. Every male must be circumcised. The sign is in the body. The penalty for refusing is exclusion.

**Movement Three (vv. 15–22 / vv. 23–27): Promise of Isaac / Immediate obedience.** Sarah is renamed and promised. Abraham laughs. Abraham pleads for Ishmael. God holds both: blessing for Ishmael, covenant through Isaac. And then — the very same day — Abraham obeys.

The chapter is a covenant document in narrative form. It has the structure of ancient Near Eastern suzerainty treaties: the sovereign (God) identifies himself by name and history, makes commitments, lays out obligations for the vassal (Abraham/Israel), specifies the sign of the covenant relationship, and calls for obedience. Genesis 17 follows this structure not because the biblical author was copying a legal form, but because the covenant form is the natural grammar for expressing this kind of relationship: not contract between equals, but commitment from a greater party to a dependent one, with the dependent party's response invited and expected.

---

### El Shaddai and the God of the body

There is a coherence to this chapter that only becomes visible when the details are held together.

El Shaddai — the name introduced here — is associated throughout the patriarchal narratives with blessing and fertility, with multiplication and abundance (Genesis 28:3; 35:11; 49:25). Whether or not the etymology connects to "breast" (*shad*), the semantic field is the same: this is the God who overcomes barrenness, who makes life emerge from where death seemed certain, who causes the old and the closed to bring forth. El Shaddai is the name under which God visits the impossible corners of biology and says: not yet.

And then the sign of this God's covenant is placed in the body. Specifically in the body of every male, at the organ of generation. El Shaddai — the God who multiplies — marks the instrument of multiplication. The theology and the sign are not disconnected. The God who promises descendants beyond counting demands a permanent mark on the means by which those descendants will come. The promise and the sign inhabit the same bodily territory.

This is what is meant by the intimacy of bodily covenant. Unlike the rainbow (Genesis 9), which hangs in the sky and can be seen by anyone, the sign of the Abrahamic covenant is private, hidden, visible only in contexts of vulnerability. It is carried by every male in the community without display. It is a secret sign between the body and God, between flesh and promise, a permanent reminder etched in the most tender place of what has been committed to — and by whom.

---

### The thirteen-year silence: what it means for Abraham

One more thing must be said about the gap.

Between Genesis 16:16 and Genesis 17:1, thirteen years pass without a word from God. Ishmael is born. Abram is eighty-six. He becomes ninety-nine. And the text gives us nothing of those thirteen years — no vision, no visit, no covenant renewal, no word of reassurance. Just silence and the ordinary passage of time.

This is not oversight. The biblical narrative is exquisitely selective about what it preserves. Those thirteen years are preserved as silence precisely because the silence is part of the story. Abraham lives with the child he loves, with the wife who is aging, with a promise that has not moved in more than two decades since the call of chapter 12. He has had one extraordinary covenant ceremony (Genesis 15), one divine name (YHWH, whose promises he has been waiting on), one child born not of the promise but of a human plan (Ishmael). And then: nothing. Thirteen years of ordinary life.

When God returns in chapter 17, he does not explain the silence. He simply appears and introduces himself with a new name — El Shaddai — as if to say: I am the one who can do things you have stopped expecting. Walk before me. Be whole-hearted. I am not done.

The thirteen years of silence are the years in which Abraham had every reason to conclude that Ishmael was the fulfillment, that the promise had been met, that the extraordinary was behind him. God's reappearance does not apologize for the delay. It simply overrides the conclusions that the silence might have encouraged. The appointment is still on the calendar. The time is still set. El Shaddai has not forgotten. The waiting was not abandonment; it was the long walk through ordinary time toward the moment that had always been coming.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 9v–10r, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Genesis 17 begins in the central and left-hand columns of folio 9v (page 001, which also contains the closing verses of Genesis 16) and continues through folio 10r (page 002), which carries the chapter through verse 27 and into the opening of Genesis 18. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 12–13 (pa_0012, pa_0013); the chapter header CAP. ΧΙΧ is visible at the top of the right column of pa_0012, marking the Vaticanus enumeration of this material, with the text continuing through pa_0013 where CAP. XX marks the transition into Genesis 18.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
